findings.suggestion: Do not use eval. Parse the filter with a safe, restricted evaluator (e.g. a whitelisted expression parser / jsep-style AST walker, or a sandbox like vm2 with no host access), or replace the free-form string with a structured filter object ({field, op, value}) validated against an allowlist. Wrap parsing in try/catch and surface an 'invalid filter' error state instead of letting exceptions bubble and crash the flow.
findings.summary: source.js:8 evaluates the untrusted `expression` argument with `eval('(' + expression + ')')`. Any value passed to `buildFilter` executes as JavaScript in the host process (data exfiltration via fetch, process crash/DoS, data loss). Even benign-but-malformed user filters throw an uncaught SyntaxError from eval (line 8) or at predicate-call time (line 9), so there is no graceful 'invalid filter' state — the user flow simply crashes. Export at line 12 makes this a public contract; blast-radius shows 0 callers today, so impact is latent but real once wired up.
findings.title: Arbitrary code execution via eval() on caller-supplied expression

findings.suggestion: Never eval caller input. Parse `expression` into a structured/whitelisted filter (AST or predicate DSL) and evaluate that, or use a sandboxed expression library (e.g. jsep + a safe evaluator). Reject any input that cannot be validated against an allow-list.
findings.summary: buildFilter passes its parameter directly into eval at source.js:8 — `const predicate = eval('(' + expression + ')')`. Any caller-controlled value becomes executing JavaScript (data exfiltration via fetch, process crash, full RCE). The implicit invariant that `expression` is trusted (assumed by lines 1 and 5-7) is never enforced: no allow-list, no AST parse, no type/format guard. The benign name `buildFilter` with 0 current callers means the next agent will likely pass user input into it, silently introducing RCE.
findings.title: Arbitrary code execution: eval() on caller-supplied expression

findings.suggestion: Do not mark this change verified. Replace the eval implementation per the critical finding, add a test that proves untrusted input cannot execute, and attach fresh `node --test` output before closing the gate.
findings.summary: source.js:5-7 carries a `// BUG:` comment describing the arbitrary-code-execution hole, yet the vulnerable eval was committed as-is — a comment is not a remediation. There is no test file and no command/log evidence for this diff, so the `no-fresh-verification-evidence` / `rerun_relevant_verification` gate is unresolved. The next agent cannot treat this as done.
findings.title: Incomplete handoff: known bug shipped with a comment instead of a fix, no tests/verification

nodes.label: rerun_relevant_verification

summary:
Risk-tiered review (lite) completed.

New file source.js introduces buildFilter, which passes its `expression` argument straight into eval() at line 8 — a critical arbitrary-code-execution / code-injection vulnerability. The file's own header and comment acknowledge it as a bug. From the assumption-integrity angle, the code relies on an unenforced 'expression is trusted' invariant while exposing a benignly-named function (buildFilter, 0 callers) that a future caller would naturally feed user input into, snowballing the vulnerability. Finding is statically provable from line 8; no fresh runtime evidence is required.

Single new file exporting buildFilter(), which runs caller-supplied input through eval() — arbitrary code execution plus an ungraceful crash path for malformed user filters. Critical. Currently 0 callers (newly added, unused), so live impact is latent, but the function is exported as a reusable contract and must be fixed before anything consumes it. The eval defect is provable statically; the only open verification item is a trivial one-line repro, not a blocker to confirming the finding.

Single new 12-line file introducing buildFilter, which eval()s a caller-supplied string — a critical arbitrary-code-execution vulnerability that the diff self-documents as a BUG. It was committed with a descriptive comment instead of a fix, no tests, and no verification evidence. Currently 0 callers (dead code), but exported, so the public contract is unsafe. Blocker, not shippable.
